affected_groups: Parents and pupils in general schools (day-to-day stability, retention outcomes)
Teachers (workload, compliance burden, unfunded compensatory work)
Government/ministry budget planners (fiscal sustainability, administrative deliverability)
Grammar-school (gimnázium) maintainers and the selective-track constituency (subsidiarity, track preservation)
Employers/digital-economy hiring pools relying on the competency floor of the non-tracked majority
Disadvantaged pupils dependent on TEIP-style targeted funding for compensatory support
content.decision_question: Stability and administrative/political feasibility versus the transformative ambition of structural equity reform: this cluster prioritizes deliverable, reversible, lower-risk gains over the deeper (but riskier) equalization that changing the selection age might bring.
content.evidence_boundary: Contested/uncertain on transfer validity: the Portugal figures (0.3 SD achievement gain, EUR 112m savings) are documented in that national context, but whether comparable gains materialize in Hungary's early-selection system — where compositional/selection effects on the achievement gap may be structurally different — is not established; the political-risk finding that targeted funding is a likely first budget cut is itself an empirical claim about fiscal-political behavior that is plausible but not verified for this specific policy.
content.tension: Non-structural reform (curriculum clarity, grade-retention reduction, TEIP-style targeted funding) can deliver real, evidence-backed achievement and fiscal gains (Portugal: 0.3 SD, EUR 112m savings) with lower workload, disruption, and political-reversal risk than structural reform, without touching selection age.
content.title: Decision tension A11: S4
value_poles: the transformative ambition of structural equity reform: this cluster prioritizes deliverable, reversible, lower-risk gains over the deeper (but riskier) equalization that changing the selection age might bring.
